1:
"And Abraham was old, and well stricken in age: and the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things." The witnesses note Abraham's old age and the LORD's blessing him in all things. On "Abraham was old, and well stricken in age," the grammatical voices note the years. Ellicott and Gill note that "Abraham was old, and well stricken in age" — Abraham was now advanced in years (about a hundred and forty, Isaac being forty, 25:20); the aged patriarch, near the close of his life; the setting for his care to provide a wife for Isaac (vv.2-4) before his death; the old age of the father of the faithful.

On "the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the blessing. Henry and Calvin note that "the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things" — God had blessed the patriarch in everything (in substance, in his son, in his long life, in the covenant); the fulfillment of the promise of blessing (12:2); the temporal and spiritual blessing on Abraham; the faithful God crowning his servant's life with blessing.

On the blessing, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: the LORD's blessing Abraham "in all things" sets the stage for the chapter — the aged, blessed patriarch now seeking, by God's guidance, a wife for the son of promise (that the blessing might continue in the seed); the providence of God over the household; the blessing that would be carried forward through Isaac and Rebekah. The verse displays Abraham's old age and the LORD's blessing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the age — "Abraham was old, and well stricken in age," the aged patriarch (25:20, Ellicott, Gill); "the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things," God's blessing on the patriarch (12:2, Henry, Calvin); the blessing carried forward through the seed (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The blessing displays God crowning Abraham's life. The verse displays the Abraham: old. The verse displays the LORD: blessing. The verse displays the seed: to be provided for. Abraham's old age and the LORD's blessing him in all things display the setting of the chapter: Abraham was old, and the LORD had blessed him in all things — the aged, blessed patriarch now seeking, by God's guidance, a wife for the son of promise, that the blessing (12:2) might continue in the seed. The verse displays the aged Abraham, who charges his servant (vv.2-4).
2:
"And Abraham said unto his eldest servant of his house, that ruled over all that he had, Put, I pray thee, thy hand under my thigh:" The witnesses note the oath gesture — the crux of "thy hand under my thigh." On "his eldest servant of his house, that ruled over all that he had," the grammatical voices note the servant. Ellicott and Gill note that "his eldest servant of his house, that ruled over all that he had" — Abraham charged his chief, trusted servant (the steward over all his house, generally taken to be Eliezer of Damascus, 15:2); the faithful servant entrusted with the great commission of finding a wife for Isaac; the steward of authority and trust.

On "Put, I pray thee, thy hand under my thigh," interpreters expound the gesture. The grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the variant meanings of the oath gesture: "Put... thy hand under my thigh" — a solemn form of oath (also used by Jacob, 47:29); variously understood — (a) a token of subjection, the servant swearing under his master's authority; (b) a reference to the covenant of circumcision (the "thigh"/loins a euphemism, the oath sworn by the sign of the holy covenant between Abraham and God); (c) a reference to the promised Seed — Christian writers conceive that the gesture pointed to the Messiah, the promised Seed who should come from Abraham's loins (swearing by him, or by the hope of the seed, cf. Hebrews 7:9-10); the solemn, sacred form of oath, binding the servant by what was most sacred to Abraham (the covenant and the promise).

On the oath, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the discipline: the gesture marks the solemnity of the charge — Abraham binding his servant by a sacred oath; whatever the precise meaning, the oath rested on the covenant and the promise; the gravity of the commission to find a wife for the son of promise. The verse displays the oath gesture.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abraham bade his chief servant put his hand under his thigh to swear a solemn oath (Ellicott, Keil, Gill, Calvin). The gesture bears a crux: a solemn oath-form variously understood — a token of subjection (swearing under the master's authority); or a reference to the covenant of circumcision (the sacred covenant the ground of the oath); or a reference to the promised Seed, Christ, who should come from Abraham's loins (the Christian conception). The verse displays the oath-gesture crux. The verse marks the servant: the eldest. The verse displays the debate: subjection, circumcision, or the promised Seed. The verse displays the oath: solemn. The verse displays the covenant: the ground. The gesture displays the sacred, solemn oath. The verse displays the Abraham: charging. The verse displays the servant: sworn. The verse displays the oath: sacred. The oath gesture displays a solemn charge and a crux: put thy hand under my thigh — the solemn oath-form read as a token of subjection, or a reference to the covenant of circumcision, or (the Christian conception) to the promised Seed, Christ, of Abraham's loins; the sacred oath binding the servant by what was most sacred. The verse displays the charging Abraham, forbidding a Canaanite wife (v.3).
3:
"And I will make thee swear by the LORD, the God of heaven, and the God of the earth, that thou shalt not take a wife unto my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I dwell:" The witnesses note the prohibition of a Canaanite wife — the crux of the separation. On "I will make thee swear by the LORD, the God of heaven, and the God of the earth," the grammatical voices note the oath. Gill and Calvin note that "I will make thee swear by the LORD, the God of heaven, and the God of the earth" — Abraham bound the servant by oath, swearing by the LORD (the Maker and possessor of heaven and earth, 14:22), the only proper object of an oath; the solemn swearing by the true God; the gravity of the charge.

On "thou shalt not take a wife unto my son of the daughters of the Canaanites," interpreters expound the prohibition. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the reason: Isaac was not to marry "of the daughters of the Canaanites" — because the Canaanites were under the curse (the curse of Canaan, 9:25), devoted to destruction/slavery; it would have been inconsistent with prudence and with God's design to unite the child and heir of the promise with one under a curse; the separation of the seed of promise from the accursed, idolatrous line; the avoidance of the unequal yoke (the danger of idolatry and corruption, as later forbidden, Deuteronomy 7:3); the marrying "in the faith," within the covenant family.

On "among whom I dwell," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the danger: "among whom I dwell" — though Abraham dwelt among the Canaanites, he would not have his son marry among them; the patriarch keeping the seed separate from the corrupt nations; the care to preserve the covenant-line from idolatry and the curse; the godly concern for a wife of the true faith and kindred. The verse displays the prohibition of a Canaanite wife.

The witnesses agree the substance: Abraham bound the servant by oath not to take a wife for Isaac from the Canaanites (Gill, Clarke, Calvin). The prohibition bears its weight as a crux of the separation: the Canaanites under the curse (9:25), idolatrous, devoted to destruction; the heir of promise not to be unequally yoked with the accursed line (Deuteronomy 7:3); the seed kept separate, the wife to be of the covenant kindred and faith. The verse displays the Canaanite-wife crux. The verse marks the oath: by the LORD. The verse displays the prohibition: no Canaanite wife. The verse displays the curse: of Canaan (9:25). The verse displays the separation: of the seed. The prohibition displays the seed kept from the accursed line. The verse displays the Isaac: not yoked to Canaan. The verse displays the wife: of the kindred. The verse displays the faith: preserved. The prohibition of a Canaanite wife displays the separation and a crux: thou shalt not take a wife of the Canaanites — the heir of promise not to be united with the accursed (9:25), idolatrous line (Deuteronomy 7:3), the seed kept separate, the wife to be of the covenant kindred and faith. The verse displays the separating Abraham, sending to his kindred (v.4).
4:
"But thou shalt go unto my country, and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son Isaac." The brief verse notes the charge to seek a wife from Abraham's kindred. The grammatical voices note the country and kindred. Gill and Clarke note that "thou shalt go unto my country, and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son Isaac" — Abraham charged the servant to go to his own country (Mesopotamia, Haran) and to his kindred (the family of Nahor, 22:20-23) to find a wife for Isaac; the seeking of a wife from the covenant family, not the Canaanites (v.3); the care for a wife of the true kindred and faith.

On "unto my country, and to my kindred," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the family. Calvin and Gill note that the wife was to be sought from Abraham's "country and kindred" — the family of Nahor in Mesopotamia (from whom Rebekah, the granddaughter of Nahor, would come, 22:23); the seeking within the family that, though imperfect (Laban's idolatry, 31:19), retained some knowledge of the true God; the wife of the kindred, kept from the Canaanite line.

On "take a wife unto my son Isaac," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the purpose: "take a wife unto my son Isaac" — the commission to find a wife for the son of promise; the providence of God to be sought in the matter (vv.7, 12-14); the marriage of Isaac, through which the covenant-line would continue; the care of the godly father for the marriage of his son in the faith. The verse displays the charge to seek a wife from Abraham's kindred.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "thou shalt go unto my country, and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son Isaac," the seeking of a wife from the kindred (22:20-23, Gill, Clarke); "unto my country, and to my kindred," the family of Nahor (22:23, Calvin, Gill); "take a wife unto my son Isaac," the marriage of the son of promise (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charging Abraham, answered by the servant's question (v.5).
5:
"And the servant said unto him, Peradventure the woman will not be willing to follow me unto this land: must I needs bring thy son again unto the land from whence thou camest?" The witnesses note the servant's prudent question about the woman's willingness. On "Peradventure the woman will not be willing to follow me unto this land," the grammatical voices note the foresight. Gill and Calvin note that "the servant said... Peradventure the woman will not be willing to follow me unto this land" — the servant prudently raised the possibility that the woman might refuse to leave her home and follow him to Canaan; the faithful servant's careful foresight, considering the difficulties of his commission; the prudent inquiry before undertaking the oath.

On "must I needs bring thy son again unto the land," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the question. Clarke and Gill note that the servant asked, "must I needs bring thy son again unto the land from whence thou camest?" — if the woman would not come, must Isaac go to her (back to Mesopotamia)? the careful working out of the terms of the commission; the servant seeking clear instructions; the question that drew out Abraham's firm answer (vv.6-8).

On the question, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the care: the servant's question shows his fidelity and prudence — he would understand his charge fully before swearing; the careful, conscientious servant; the foresight that anticipated difficulties; the question answered by Abraham's insistence that Isaac must not return (vv.6-8). The verse displays the servant's prudent question.

6:
"And Abraham said unto him, Beware thou that thou bring not my son thither again." The very brief verse notes Abraham's firm charge not to take Isaac back. The grammatical voices note the prohibition. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Abraham said unto him, Beware thou that thou bring not my son thither again" — Abraham firmly forbade the servant to take Isaac back to Mesopotamia (whatever the woman's choice); the emphatic charge ("beware"); the patriarch's insistence that the son of promise remain in the land of promise; the care to keep Isaac in Canaan.

On "Beware thou," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the emphasis. JFB and Calvin note the emphatic warning, "beware" — Abraham strongly charged the servant on no account to take Isaac out of the land; the firmness of the patriarch; the importance of keeping the heir in the land of promise.

On "bring not my son thither again," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the reason: "bring not my son thither again" — Isaac was not to leave Canaan (the land of promise, given to the seed, v.7); to take him back to Mesopotamia would be to forsake the land of promise and endanger the covenant-walk; the care to keep the son of promise in the promised land, in separation from the kindred's idolatry; the faith that held Isaac to the land God had given. The verse displays Abraham's firm charge not to take Isaac back.

7:
"The LORD God of heaven, which took me from my father's house, and from the land of my kindred, and which spake unto me, and that sware unto me, saying, Unto thy seed will I give this land; he shall send his angel before thee, and thou shalt take a wife unto my son from thence." The witnesses note Abraham's faith that God would send his angel before the servant. On "The LORD God of heaven, which took me from my father's house," the grammatical voices note the recollection. Gill and Clarke note that "the LORD God of heaven, which took me from my father's house, and from the land of my kindred, and which spake unto me, and that sware unto me, saying, Unto thy seed will I give this land" — Abraham recalled God's call (12:1), his word, and his oath (the promise of the land to his seed, 12:7; 15:18; 13:15); the ground of his confidence; the God who had led and promised would guide the matter.

On "he shall send his angel before thee," interpreters note the providence. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note that "he shall send his angel before thee" — Abraham's faith that God would send his angel to guide the servant and prosper his way; the providence of God ordering the marriage; the angel (whether a created angel, or the Angel of the LORD) sent to prepare the way; the confidence that God would direct the servant to the right wife.

On "thou shalt take a wife unto my son from thence," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the assurance: "thou shalt take a wife unto my son from thence" — Abraham was assured that the servant would succeed (God would guide him to a wife from the kindred); the faith resting on God's promise and providence; the certainty of the godly that God would order the matter for the good of the covenant-seed. The verse displays Abraham's faith that God would send his angel.

8:

Gill notes that "if the woman will not be willing to follow thee, then thou shalt be clear from this my oath" — Abraham provided that, if the woman refused to come, the servant would be released from his oath; the just, reasonable condition; the servant not bound to the impossible; the careful framing of the oath.

On "thou shalt be clear from this my oath," the witnesses note the discharge. Gill notes that the servant would be "clear" (discharged, free) from the oath if the woman would not follow; the reasonable limit of the obligation; the conscientious framing of the charge, that the servant be not bound beyond what was possible.

On "only bring not my son thither again," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the one firm point: "only bring not my son thither again" — whatever happened, the one absolute condition was that Isaac must not be taken back to Mesopotamia (v.6); the firm insistence on keeping the son in the land of promise; the single unalterable term; the care to keep Isaac in Canaan. The verse displays the release of the servant if the woman refuse.

The verse displays the servant taking the oath.

10:

The witnesses note the servant's journey to the city of Nahor. On "the servant took ten camels of the camels of his master, and departed," the grammatical voices note the preparation. Ellicott and Gill note that "the servant took ten camels of the camels of his master, and departed; for all the goods of his master were in his hand" — the servant took ten camels (for the journey, the gifts, and to bring back the bride and her company) and set out, with his master's goods (gifts and provisions, vv.22, 53) at his disposal; the well-furnished journey; the steward entrusted with his master's wealth for the mission.

On "all the goods of his master were in his hand," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the trust. Henry and Gill note that "all the goods of his master were in his hand" — the servant had charge of (a portion of) his master's goods, to provide gifts for the bride and her family (vv.22, 53); the trust reposed in the faithful steward; the means for the honorable suit.

On "went to Mesopotamia, unto the city of Nahor," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the destination: "went to Mesopotamia, unto the city of Nahor" — the servant journeyed to Mesopotamia (Aram-naharaim, the land between the rivers), to the city of Nahor (Haran, or a city where Nahor's family dwelt); the journey to the kindred (vv.3-4); the providence guiding him to the family of Nahor (Rebekah's people, 22:20-23). The verse displays the servant's journey to the city of Nahor.

11:
"And he made his camels to kneel down without the city by a well of water at the time of the evening, even the time that women go out to draw water." The witnesses note the servant halting at the well at the time of drawing water. On "he made his camels to kneel down without the city by a well of water," the grammatical voices note the halt. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he made his camels to kneel down without the city by a well of water" — the servant rested his camels (made them kneel, as camels do) outside the city, by the well; the well (the place where the women came to draw water) a natural place to meet the daughters of the city; the prudent choice of place.

On "at the time of the evening, even the time that women go out to draw water," the reformed Ellicott and witnesses note the time. Ellicott and Gill note that the servant came "at the time of the evening, even the time that women go out to draw water" — the evening, when the women came to the well to draw water (the custom of the East); the prudent timing, that he might meet the maidens of the city; the place and time chosen to find a wife.

On the halt, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the providence: the servant's halting at the well, at the time of drawing water, sets the scene for the providential meeting (vv.12-15); the place where, by God's guiding, he would find Rebekah; the prudent arrangement joined with prayer (v.12) and trust in providence. The verse displays the servant halting at the well.

12:
"And he said, O LORD God of my master Abraham, I pray thee, send me good speed this day, and shew kindness unto my master Abraham." The witnesses note the servant's prayer for good speed. On "O LORD God of my master Abraham," the grammatical voices note the address. Gill and Calvin note that "he said, O LORD God of my master Abraham" — the servant prayed to the LORD, the God of his master Abraham (the true God, whom Abraham served); the faithful servant a man of prayer, seeking God's help in his task; the addressing of God as Abraham's God (the covenant God); the piety of the steward.

On "send me good speed this day," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the petition. Wesley and Gill note that the servant prayed, "send me good speed this day" — that God would prosper his way, give him good success in his errand; the seeking of God's guidance and blessing on the day's work; the prayer of one who would follow providence and seek God's direction; the dependence of the servant on God.

On "shew kindness unto my master Abraham," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the ground: "shew kindness unto my master Abraham" — the servant pleaded God's kindness to Abraham (for his master's sake, in fulfillment of the promise); the prayer grounded on God's covenant love to Abraham; the seeking of God's mercy and faithfulness; the servant's care for his master's interest, and his faith in God's covenant kindness. The verse displays the servant's prayer for good speed.

On the asking of a sign, interpreters weigh its propriety. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the dominant reading hold that the servant's asking a sign was lawful faith and prudent prayer, not a presumptuous tempting of God: it is the comfort and belief of a good man that God's providence extends to the smallest occurrences; it is lawful, while we set God's will before us as our rule, to pray that God would, by hints of providence, direct us in the way of our duty; the servant did not rashly tempt God, but, in dependence and prayer, sought God's guidance by a sign that itself tested the bride's fitness; the prayer of faith following providence. A more cautious view notes that such signs are not to be lightly multiplied (lest we tempt God), and that this was an extraordinary case, guided by the Spirit; but the servant's sign, joined with prayer and reason, is generally approved as faith, not presumption.
